concept_ref: §3 row 3 — 'Decision coverage = Branch Condition coverage' (corrected by §5.3.4)

scenario.title: Two-Factor Login
scenario.narrative: A login function admits a user only when they are logged in AND have permission. Two tests are submitted.

claim: Which is the STRONGEST coverage level the test set below actually satisfies?

options.label: Branch Condition (each condition T and F)

hints: Decision testing only asks: did the if-statement produce both TRUE and FALSE outcomes overall? Branch Condition asks something stricter — re-read Law Card 4.
Look at A and B separately. Did A appear as both TRUE and FALSE across the tests? What about B? Are any of them stuck on a single value?

Solution — Decision is 100% (T1 → true outcome, T2 → false outcome). But Branch Condition is NOT satisfied: A and B always move together. We never see A=T, B=F (or A=F, B=T). Strongest satisfied = DECISION.

seeded_faults.description: Permission check is silently skipped — a user with is_logged_in=True is granted access regardless of has_permission.

misconceptions.explanation_md: Decision coverage tests only the overall outcome of the predicate (T/F at the if-statement). Branch Condition coverage requires *each individual condition* to take both TRUE and FALSE across the test set. With T1=(T,T) and T2=(F,F), A and B move together — a real-world bug where the permission check is bypassed when the user is logged in stays hidden.
